entry.title: When was the last time that you upgraded your supply chain planning system?
entry.content:
While in private, we swiftly apply the latest app updates and from time to time even switch out our old smartphones, once they become incompatible with the latest operating system, it's curious how we sometimes forget one of the most critical upgrades – the systems that drive our businesses.

Nowadays, maintaining a supply chain with outdated technology can be as frustrating as using a navigation system from the early 2000s (remember? we used to have dedicated devices for that, not our smartphones): you might get served by the technology, but you'll hardly be wowed by it. Most likely, the maps aren’t even up to date anymore; they may lead you into dead ends - or the wrong direction!
It’s time to throw away the CDs with the map updates and move to a modern, state-of-the-art system that comes with regular, automated enhancements.

Working in Supply Chain for more than a decade and with many businesses from all the different industries, let me tell you why it's time to revisit your planning application, particularly if you’re an SAP APO (Advanced Planner & Optimizer) user. The business environment and -models have changed and still do, and so should the systems that support you.
Switching from APO to SAP Integrated Business Planning (IBP) isn't just another technical upgrade; it's a step towards the future of planning, and it will change what your business can  to do – beyond what you think of as Supply Chain Planning.

Why It's Time to Move from SAP APO to SAP IBP

If you’ve been running SAP APO for years, you know how reliable and robust the system is. It does a great job, and it got many of us through some very turbulent times. But the way we plan has changed - faster cycles, more volatility, more data, and more stakeholders asking, “Can we commit?” in hours or even minutes, not days. Planning has moved from periodic, forecast-centric cycles to continuous, data-driven decisions with real-time visibility. If your team is still holding its breath for nightly batch runs, that’s your sign. Here's why you should make that leap:

  •                Planning now needs to be much faster and more agile. You need to sense demand shifts quickly, replan in minutes, and commit orders with confidence. APO’s architecture was built for time-series forecasts and batch optimization; today’s reality demands order-level agility and instant simulation.
  •                Your execution is already in S/4HANA (or heading there). The strongest planning outcomes come from tight integration with aATP, PP/DS, TM, EWM, and finance. Keeping APO in the middle adds latency, mappings, and maintenance you no longer need.
  •                Your planners need one place to decide, simulate, and align. SAP IBP gives them an exception-driven workspace, scenario management, and “what-if” results.
  •                Data is bigger, messier, and external. Weather, promotions, POS, IoT, supplier confirmations – SAP IBP is built to ingest and act on it.

Where are the SAP APO Modules going?

The “network centric” planning parts of SAP APO, Demand Planning and Supply Network Planning, will be covered by SAP IBP, and enhanced with a lot of more capabilities that I will mention in the next part.

The plant- centric parts have moved into SAP S/4HANA for tighter execution integration.

And SAP IBP is not a 1:1 exchange for SAP APO. It comes with a long list of advantages:

  1. Real-time planning and analytics without data replication
    SAP IBP offers real-time planning and analytics, enabling faster decision-making compared to SAP APO. It offers advanced scenario management and simulation for efficient exploration of planning strategies. Additionally, it enables data aggregation and drill-down, providing detailed insights into business operations.
    Furthermore, it not only plans on units, but can convert these numbers into monetary value – allowing for decision making based on revenue and cost, not on volume. Advanced analytics help with visualization and evaluation.
  2. Collaboration and process support
    While APO was a solid planning tool, it was hard to  collaborate across the enterprise. However, to create the best possible plan and to agree upon that, a lot of stakeholders need to work together. SAP IBP comes with its own set of process and decision support, review cycles, and a collaboration platform. Process steps can be automated, and alerts allow for management by exception
  3. A true and native cloud environment
    with all the associated benefits. Regular updates and new functionality, better scalability, browser-based access, integration with other tools. Delivering more flexibility & agility to succeed in an extremely dynamic business environment.
  4. Internal and external integration
    SAP IBP is an end-to-end tool for supply chain planning, covering demand, inventory and supply planning, from strategic over tactical to operational level. Simulating an increase in demand? You can immediately see the effects on the inventory and supply side.
    Moreover, integration with other systems such as the Business Network, SAC or SAP S/4 HANA allow for processes and collaboration across tools, departments and even companies.
  5. New set of functionality
    SAP IBP offers a whole set of capabilities that SAP APO did not offer.
    Starting from true S&OP process support, over Demand Sensing, Multi Stage Inventory Optimization, extensive Scenario and Simulation capabilities…and many more. We will deeper dive into this topic on the next blog.
  6. Embedded AI
    Last but not least, of course there is extensive AI functionality built into the tool. From the conversational agent, Joule, that helps planners through a chat function, over advanced Planning Assistance, to AI-assisted optimization analysis for better understanding and evaluation of forecasting & optimization results, there is a broad range of use cases covered. Not as an add-on, but truly embedded.

In summary, SAP IBP is not just an upgrade but a mature platform leveraging the latest technologies to deliver a future proof planning platform. The world of supply chain planning has moved to the next stage, and SAP IBP combines best-of-breed with best-of suite advantages. As businesses embrace new technologies to keep pace with the market, clinging to APO is like holding on to your old navigation device. At best, it might lead you onto a very bumpy road.
It's time to let go of the old and embrace the new; update your supply chain planning tool to unleash you full business potential.

Stay tuned for the next part where we'll explore why choosing SAP IBP over other supply chain planning solutions is your best strategic move, bringing capabilities you never imagined were possible.

If in the meantime, you want to learn more, you can visit:

  • Modern Supply Chain Planning – a Strategic Guide
  • Unlock your supply chain potential with SAP Integrated Business Planning AI
    to learn about AI in SAP IBP
  • What is SAP Integrated Business Planning?
  • SAP IBP webcasts
  • A Predictable Path to IBP: Proven Approaches from the SAP Partner Ecosystem
    Whitepaper

This blog is AI infused 😉

entry.title: Integration of Position Budgeting Control for Cloud with SAP S/4HANA On-Premise Payroll
entry.content:
Position Budgeting Control (PBC) for Cloud enables organizations to reserve and control workforce budget across fiscal years and budget periods. PBC acts as a bridge between SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central and SAP S/4HANA Financials, ensuring that payroll costs are aligned with approved budgets. This blog describes how to integrate PBC with SAP S/4HANA Payroll in an on-premise or private cloud deployment.

Note: SAP provides standard integration with Employee Central Payroll on the modern stack only. This guide describes the steps to integrate PBC with SAP S/4HANA Payroll. Custom iFlow development and any issues arising from non-standard integration configurations are the responsibility of the implementing partner or customer.

Prerequisites

Before you begin, you must complete the setup and configuration of Position Budgeting Control for Cloud.

High-Level Overview of the PBC Integration to SAP S/4HANA Payroll

Figure 1 Block diagram showing the integration architecture for SAP S/4HANA On-Premise Payroll, highlighting which components are part of the standard integration and which require custom development.

Integrating PBC with SAP S/4HANA Payroll (On-Premise/Private Cloud)

For SAP S/4HANA On-Premise Payroll, the standard synchronization job is not available. Instead, you must build custom iFlows to handle synchronization of symbolic account and employee grouping data, and to communicate with the earmarked funds API.

Build Custom iFlows for Symbolic Accounts and Employee Groupings

The symbolic account and employee grouping services cannot use standard communication scenarios for on-premise systems and must be handled via custom iFlows. Create custom iFlows to consume the following standard OData services delivered by SAP:

  • HRSFEC_SYMBOLIC_ACCOUNT_SRV – Fetches symbolic account data from payroll.
  • HRSFEC_EMPLOYEE_GROUP_SRV – Fetches employee grouping data for account determination.

Activate the services in your SAP S/4HANA On-Premise system and enable them with Basic Auth or OAuth-based configuration.

End-to-End iFlow Implementation

In this integration, a single iFlow calls both OData services sequentially within SAP Integration Suite (CPI), captures each response, and combines the data before returning the final response to PBC.

Overview

  • User triggers the iFlow using an HTTPS sender.
  • The iFlow calls HRSFEC_SYMBOLIC_ACCOUNT_SRV, captures its response.
  • The iFlow then calls HRSFEC_EMPLOYEE_GROUP_SRV in a similar manner.
  • The combined response is returned.

Step 1: Create an iFlow

Create a new Integration Flow in SAP Integration Suite with the following structure:

HTTPS sender → iFlow logic → HTTP receiver

Step 2: Configure the HTTPS Sender Adapter

In the sender adapter, set the address in the Connection tab (endpoint path). This endpoint is what you will configure later in Security Center for symbolic account and employee grouping. For example: /SymbolicAccount. The address must start with /.

Step 3: Configure the Content Modifier

Add a Content Modifier step and configure the required exchange properties for the API calls (such as the target system URL and authentication parameters).

Step 4: Set the ERP Configuration

Configure the connection details pointing to your SAP S/4HANA On-Premise system.

Step 5: Add a Request Reply Step

Add a Request Reply step to handle the outbound HTTP call to the on-premise OData service.

Step 6: Configure the HTTP Adapter (SAP S/4HANA On-Premise System)

Configure the HTTP receiver adapter with the endpoint of the on-premise OData service (HRSFEC_SYMBOLIC_ACCOUNT_SRV or HRSFEC_EMPLOYEE_GROUP_SRV) and the appropriate authentication method.

Repeat Steps 3–6 to add the second OData service call (HRSFEC_EMPLOYEE_GROUP_SRV) within the same iFlow.

Step 7: Save and Deploy the iFlow

Save and deploy the iFlow in SAP Integration Suite. After deployment, verify the status in the Integration Content Monitor — the iFlow should show as Started. Any startup errors will be visible here before any message processing log is created.

Step 8: Test the iFlow

Test the iFlow using the service bindings created for the instance. Confirm that both OData services return data as expected.

After building and testing the iFlows, configure the synchronization job with the iFlow endpoint details and register the iFlows in Security Center, as shown in Configuring the Integration Content for the Earmarked Funds API and Configuring the X509 Certificate in the Integration Flow.

Alternative approach: Instead of building custom iFlows to retrieve the symbolic account and employee grouping data, you can use the Manage Symbolic Accounts and Manage Employee Groupings APIs during configuration to create and save the mapping between the Pay Components ID to Symbolic Accounts ID and Employee Class and Employment Type to Employee Grouping for account determination. The symbolic accounts and employee groupings from the payroll systems are used by Position Budgeting and Control to derive the G/L account information required for the Financials system in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

These APIs expose the same data and can be used when a custom iFlow is not preferred:

  • Symbolic Accounts API
  • Employee Groupings API

Enable PBC in SAP S/4HANA Payroll (On-Premise/Private Cloud)

Register the symbolic account and account determination APIs configured in the previous steps. The enabling, registering, and setup steps may differ from the standard Employee Central Payroll steps detailed in the Implementing Employee Central Payroll based on SAP Human Capital Management for SAP S/4HANA guide.

Perform IT724 Replication

Ensure the PTP Replication connection is configured using report RP_HRSFEC_PTP_CONFIGURATION as a prerequisite. Then schedule report RP_HRSFEC_PBC_REPL (transaction HRSFEC_PBC_REPL) as a periodic job in the payroll system. This report fetches financial status and line items via the PBC API and replicates the data to IT724 in your payroll system. Application logs are written under object HRSFEC, sub-object PBC, with external ID prefix IT724_REPLICATION*.

entry.title: Ask Me Anything with SAP Champions – Monthly Series (Session 5)
entry.content:
Hi folks, Martin here 👋
 
🎬We're back — and this time we're getting our hands dirty with security.
 
The Ask Me Anything with SAP Champions series is rolling into Session 5, and I'm excited to be in the hot seat this time. The concept is dead simple: you ask, I answer. No slides, no scripted demos, no marketing gloss — just honest answers to the questions you actually care about.
 
And the topic? The one that keeps SAP architects, Basis admins, and CISOs up at night: SAP Security 🛡️
 
Securing S/4HANA, RISE, GROW, BTP, threat detection, identity and access, integration security, and the brand-new headache of AI agents poking around in your SAP data — if it's about keeping SAP safe, it's fair game.

🤔So what should you ask me?

Honestly? Whatever's bugging you. But if you want a head start, here's what fits this session perfectly:
 
- 🔐 Securing SAP in the cloud — RISE, S/4HANA, BTP and the works
- 👀 Threat detection & monitoring — actually *seeing* what's happening inside SAP
- 🪪 Identity & access — SSO, principal propagation, federation, least privilege done right
- 🔗 Integration security — APIs, OData, BTP, connecting SAP to the outside world without leaving the door open
- 🤖 AI & automation — keeping copilots and agents that read SAP data on a tight leash
- 🚀 Getting started — practical first steps when you're maturing your SAP security posture

 

Don't see your topic? Ask anyway. The questions I have to *think* about are always the best ones — and far more fun than the easy ones. 😉

📨How to send me your questions (pick whatever's easiest)

Three ways in, all roads lead to the same place:
 
1. Microsoft Forms — name or community handle optional, ask anonymously if you like 👉 link
2. Comment right here — drop your question under this blog post
3. LinkedIn — reply to my announcement post 👉 [LinkedIn post link — placeholder]

 

🗓️ Get them in by: July 6th

🎥What happens next

Here's the neat part — there's nothing to attend, nothing to squeeze into your calendar:

 

1. You send your questions before the deadline.
2. I pick the most interesting and widely useful ones.
3. I answer them on camera in a recorded video on the SAP Community YouTube channel 📺
4. And for the readers among you (I see you 👀), a written recap blog with all the questions and answers lands right here afterwards.
 
So whether you prefer to watch or read, you get the full thing on your own schedule. 🙌

💡Why bother joining in?

- Get a real answer to a real problem — bring me the thing you're genuinely stuck on.
- Learn from questions you'd never have thought to ask — this community always surfaces angles I love.
- Steer the series — your questions shape what we tackle next.
 

👉Over to you

SAP security isn't a one-and-done checkbox — it's an ongoing conversation. So let's have it.
 
Fire your question over via anonymous Forms, drop it in the comments, or hit me up on [LinkedIn — placeholder] before July 6th.
 
I'll see you in the recording — and in the recap blog right here on the community. Let's go! 🚀
 
See the other series editions in the community corner and where it all began here.
 
#SAPChampions #AMA #SAPCommunity #SAPSecurity #SAPCyberDefense

entry.title: Re: Custom Root Entity Use case along with custom messages in RAP
entry.content:
@Joery - 

Yes, it's more of an interface that only supports GET operations (an outbound interface). One of its biggest advantages is that you can validate the input and control the response before displaying the data (for example, when the user clicks the Go button), which isn't possible in standard Managed or Unmanaged RAP scenarios.

If I directly map my interface view to a database table, I lose that level of control. For example, if the table contains no  data, the application simply displays a generic "No data found................." message. The same message is also shown when the user enters an invalid filter value. As a result, users cannot distinguish whether the issue is due to invalid input or because no records exist. With a Custom Entity, you can implement your own validation logic and provide meaningful, context-specific error messages.

entry.title: KT_INC10197128_Line Item Display Not Enabled for GL 21180100 in S/4HANA
entry.content:
Dear SAP Team,
We would like to seek your guidance regarding the Line Item Display configuration for GL Account 21180100 in our S/4HANA environment.
The business team observed, through a comparison in SE16N, that Line Item Display is enabled for other GL accounts, whereas it is not enabled for GL Account 21180100 across multiple company codes. Based on this comparison, the business has requested that Line Item Display be enabled for this GL account.
During our analysis, we found that Line Item Display is currently not enabled for GL Account 21180100. We also reviewed the configuration and observed the following:
The GL account is configured as a Clearing Balance Account and is assigned in the Document Splitting configuration.
As per SAP standard configuration, Line Item Display/Open Item Management is not required for this GL account because no direct postings are made to it. The system automatically derives the postings during document splitting.
In our ECC system, there was an option to activate or deactivate Line Item Display for this account. During the SPS upgrade, Open Item Management was deactivated for this GL account to avoid document posting errors.
Since this account has now been migrated to the S/4HANA e

entry.title: Your Voice on the Future of SAP UI Technology
entry.content:
AI is reshaping how enterprise applications are built, used, and experienced. This raises fundamental questions for SAP's UI technology strategy - and we want to hear from you.

TL;DR: We are running a short, anonymous survey on the future of enterprise UI development with SAP. It takes 5 minutes and is open from July 1 to July 19, 2026. Take the survey →

What are we asking?

We have put together a short survey on the future of enterprise UI development with SAP. It covers topics such as:

  • How well SAPUI5 meets your needs today - and in the age of AI
  • Which UI experiences you expect to dominate enterprise apps in the coming years
  • Which capabilities matter most for your future development work
  • Where you see SAP's UI technology strategy heading

Why your input matters

SAP's UI frameworks power thousands of enterprise applications worldwide. Decisions about their future direction affect you — whether you are building new apps, maintaining existing ones, or advising customers on their architecture. Your perspective as a developer, architect, or decision maker in the SAP ecosystem is what we need to make the right calls.

Who should take this survey?

This survey is for anyone working with SAP UI technologies: SAPUI5 / Fiori developers, UI and UX architects, technical leads, enterprise architects, and IT decision makers at SAP customers and partners.

Call to Action

Make your voice heard by participating in the survey here: SAP UI Technology Strategy Survey. It only takes 5 minutes to complete, and is open from July 1 to July 19, 2026.

entry.title: Re: SAP Business One – Set Attachment Browse Dialog to Op...
entry.content: Since SAP Business One version 10.0 FP 2602, it has been possible to drag and drop attachments into the SAP window. Users can keep the folder containing the attachments open in Windows Explorer and simply drag the files from that folder into SAP using their mouse multiple times a day. This eliminates the need to search for the correct folder.

entry.title: Re: Compatibility Pack: How to check it in your system going forward?
entry.content:
hi Prinya, 

compatibility pack items only exist in an S/4HANA system, not in an ECC system.  If a customer moves their S/4HANA system from on-premise to SAP Cloud ERP Private (formerly known as RISE), they are allowed to use all compatibility pack items until the end of 2033.  In your example this means that LE-WM can be used until the end of 2033 on any release of S/4HANA.  This is described in SAP note 2269324.

kind regards, Jurgen

entry.title: Re: SAP Ariba Certification Roadmap for Beginners (Studen...
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

I've been digging into this same question as a Learning Hub subscriber, so sharing what I've pieced together so far — happy to be corrected by anyone with real Ariba project experience.

1. Where to start as a beginner:
The SAP Learning Journey for Ariba Procurement (on learning.sap.com) seems to be the natural entry point — it covers the core buying/guided purchasing workflows before anything else. If your interest leans more toward sourcing/RFx work, there's a separate journey for that, so it's worth deciding early which track (Procurement vs. Sourcing) matches the role you're aiming for, since the content diverges fairly quickly.

2. Foundation-level certification
From what I've found, the Ariba Procurement associate-level certification is generally treated as the entry point, covering buying, catalog, and guided purchasing workflows. I'd recommend confirming the exact current certification code/name directly on the Learning Hub or SAP's Certification catalog, since these get updated periodically.

3/4. After the beginner cert — intermediate/advanced sequence
I've seen people mention a progression like Procurement → Sourcing → Integration (or Supplier Lifecycle & Performance, depending on specialization) — but I'm not sure if that's an officially prescribed SAP path or just how the market tends to hire. Would love input from someone who's actually gone through it.

5. What employers actually value
From what I can tell, employers weight hands-on project/implementation experience heavily alongside the cert — the cert alone rarely seals it. I'd also say: don't rely purely on memorizing dumps — SAP exams increasingly lean toward scenario-based questions, so hands-on sandbox practice matters.

Would really appreciate hearing from anyone who's completed the full Ariba track on how accurate this sequence is in practice, and what they'd do differently starting out.

Thanks!

entry.title: Re: Complete Beginner Seeking Guidance to Become an SAP A...
entry.content:
Hi, and welcome to the community!

I'm also learning via the Student Edition, so take this as a fellow-learner's perspective rather than an expert one.

Suggested starting sequence:

  1. "Introducing SAP ABAP Platform Fundamentals" — this looks like the right entry point on SAP Learning to get the basic architecture/concepts before touching code.
  2. "Learning Basic ABAP Programming" course — covers core syntax, and its prerequisite is genuinely just elementary knowledge of any modern language (Java, JavaScript, C#, Python), so if you've done any coding in your engineering degree you're fine to start here.
  3. "Acquiring Core ABAP Skills" learning journey — this is the deeper journey that gets into the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP) and Clean Core principles, which is the modern/cloud-relevant way ABAP is taught now rather than older on-premise-only approaches.

A note on prerequisites: some of these journeys list "Exploring SAP Business Technology Platform" as a recommended (not always mandatory) prior course, so it might be worth skimming that first for context on where ABAP fits in SAP's broader stack.

Hands-on practice:
I don't have a verified, current recommendation for a specific free sandbox/trial system name — this changes over time, so I'd suggest checking the SAP Learning Hub's own environment access (usually bundled with Student Edition) and the SAP Developer Center's tutorials/CodeJam exercises, which are hands-on by design.

General tips I've picked up:

  • Get comfortable with SAP GUI navigation and the ABAP Data Dictionary early — a lot of beginner confusion comes from environment navigation, not the language itself.
  • ABAP now spans both classic (on-prem) and Cloud/RAP-based development — worth knowing early which one your target employer actually uses, since study time differs.

Would be great to hear from working ABAP developers on what they'd add or correct here — especially on practice environments.

Thanks!

entry.title: Does SAP HANA Keep Partition Locations Across Nodes After Import?
entry.content:
Hello,

I am planning to change the schema of a large partitioned table in SAP HANA.

The system is configured in a scale-out environment, and I am considering using the export/import method.

For example, in the 'Products' table:

Partitions 1 and 5 are located on node 1
Partitions 2 and 3 are located on node 2
Partition 4 is located on node 3

In this scenario, is there a way to preserve the partition placement across nodes during the export/import process?

If so, could you please advise on the recommended approach or best practices?

Thank you.

entry.title: Commitment Plan for Asset Purchase Contracts
entry.content:
Hi all,

Working with a client in a brownfield project rise and have gone live since 1st January 2026 with active FM and AVC. Client utilises heavily a process of capturing budget during contract set up with a commitment plan (configured to a specific reservation document type where editing it is only allowed via the contract) and follow a process to close the existing PR when supplier is found (this is mainly for big amount purchases where it takes a substantial time to identify supplier). So for general goods the client follows this process and the earmarking document is used in the consequent chain of PR/PO etc. Now the client wants to also follow this exact commitment plan process during contract set-up for Assets. However the MM contract layout calls for the balance sheet account of the asset reconciliation account and consequently the commitment plan cannot be done. Any suggestion of how to enter a commitment plan for assets during the contract set-up?

entry.title: Variable Pay -why has the Target Incentive Amount doubled and the Business Performance Score halved?
entry.content:
Looking for the Variable Pat gurus!

We have a problem with one of our VP templates this year whereby the first time forms are launched for the template, the Target Incentive Amount and Business Performance Score are correct according to our business goals and formulas, however if you delete the worksheets and then re-launch, the Target Incentive Amount doubles and the Business Performance Score is half of what it should be. We thought this template must be corrupt so we rebuilt from scratch which worked as expected during the initial launch, however the issue re-occurred when we deleted and re-launched the worksheets.

For example, if business goal is loaded at 108%, the business score we see in the worksheet is 54% which is exactly half.

Has anyone seen this issue before?

entry.title: Re: MRP compensation strategy not working
entry.content:
Why MRP is not offsetting VSFB with dependent requirements is because

  • Strategy 70 (Planning at assembly level) is designed for planning finished assemblies without exploding requirements down to components until a sales order exists.
  • The consumption indicator = 2 (consumption by requirements) means planned independent requirements (PIRs/VSFB) should be consumed by sales orders, not by dependent requirements.
  • Dependent requirements (OrdRes) generated from BOM explosion are not “consuming” PIRs by default. They are treated as separate demand streams.

So even though you expect the dependent requirements to offset the PIRs, the system is behaving correctly according to standard SAP logic: PIRs are consumed only by customer requirements (sales orders), not by dependent requirements.

So in short, SAP standard MRP does not perform the “compensation” you are expecting because dependent requirements are not designed to consume PIRs. If this offsetting is a business requirement, you will need to either change the planning strategy or implement a custom enhancement.

entry.title: Re: Down payment scenario in France - Final Invoice lower...
entry.content:
Good day @mikolajmocek24 

Thank you for your question.

Based on the accounting entries shown in you screenshots, the system is processing the settlement according to the standard design of scope item 7S7.

First, the VAT calculation should be clarified:

  • EUR 36 gross at 20% VAT contains EUR 30 net and EUR 6 VAT.
  • EUR 24 gross contains EUR 20 net and EUR 4 VAT.
  • EUR 4.80 would apply if EUR 24 were the net amount, which is not what your final invoice screenshot shows.

When the EUR 36 down payment was received, the system posted VAT of EUR 6. The final invoice then settled only EUR 24 of that down payment. Your final invoice contains both a EUR 4 output VAT posting and a corresponding EUR 4 down payment tax settlement posting. Therefore, the final invoice does not create an additional net VAT amount of EUR 4.

The remaining EUR 12 consists of EUR 10 net and EUR 2 VAT. Note that during advanced down payment processing, received down payments or portions of them are reserved and settled against the relevant invoice items when the invoice is posted to accounting.

See the following reference for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a376cd9ea00d476b96f18dea1247e6a5/665bf0be47984db7a2db3c34d482ea7a.html?locale=en-US

So, in simple terms:

  • The final invoice cannot change the original EUR 36 receipt into a EUR 24 receipt.
  • The invoice settles EUR 24.
  • EUR 12 remains as an unused customer down payment.
  • The VAT remains EUR 6 while the company still holds the complete EUR 36.
  • The VAT cannot be reduced to EUR 4 by changing only the final invoice.

I recommend you check the customer account as follows:

Open Manage Customer Line Items, enter the company code and customer.  Select both open and cleared items.

Include special G/L transactions. Search for the down payment and final invoice. The EUR 24 final invoice should be settled and EUR 12 remains as an open down payment credit.

If another sale will follow, the remaining down payment can be used against a subsequent invoice. See KBA 3693440 for more details.

If no further sale will follow, a separate refund or reversal of the excess EUR 12 is required. The final invoice itself cannot perform this correction. Do not post a manual adjustment directly to the VAT account.

The reason is, the exact VAT correction for refunding the excess depends on the France localisation and the payment reversal method used, the available 7S7 documentation does not provide a verified tenant procedure that guarantees automatic reversal of the EUR 2 VAT. Therefore, you should test the refund or reversal in your quality tenant and, if the EUR 2 is not reversed correctly, submit a support request to SAP.

In Summary:

The current final invoice settlement is standard. EUR 24 is settled and EUR 12 remains as a down payment. The final invoice cannot reduce the original VAT while the excess payment is retained. Use the remainder for another invoice or process a separate supported refund or reversal and validate the VAT result.

 

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: LOSC Putaway: Second WT Gets Cancelled After Confirming First WT
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

I am testing the LOSC process for putaway in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud - EWM.

Configuration

  • GR Zone → Intermediate Storage Type → Putaway Storage Type

  • Created two storage types:

    • Intermediate Storage Type

    • Putaway Storage Type

  • Configured the required storage bins and bin determination.

  • Configured a simple LOSC process.

Test Scenario

After posting the Goods Receipt,  created the putaway Warehouse Task.

As expected, the system created two Warehouse Tasks:

  • First WT – Open

  • Second WT – Waiting

However, after confirming the first WT, the second WT is cancelled automatically, and the HU remains stuck in the intermediate storage bin 

Comparison with Outbound LOSC

For the outbound LOSC scenario, I used the same intermediate storage type with the following process:

Putaway Storage Type → Intermediate Storage Type → GI Zone

In this case, the system also created two Warehouse Tasks. After confirming both tasks, the HU moved to the GI Area successfully.

I also tested an outbound LOSC process with multiple intermediate storage types, and it worked as expected.

The issue occurs only during the putaway LOSC process.

Has anyone encountered this behavior before, or is there any additional configuration required for inbound LOSC in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud EWM?

Any guidance or suggestions would be greatly appreciated.
Thank you!

entry.title: Table activation in EHS
entry.content:
Getting an error below, while trying to create PO from WAE02 disposal processing. While analysed, came to know MM-PM integration, below needs to be maintained. we are in ECC EHP7 SP16 pack. Is it possible to activate the tables or transaction WACM01/02

  1. The maintenance dialog for EHSWAC_ENTAMBW is incomplete or not defined

Message No. SV037

 

  1. Table/view EHSWAD_ENTDOC is not in the Dictionary - Message No. SV164

 

When analyses, it was came to know that the FM calls the table below :

Account Assignment Category (KNTTP) NOT set in SPRO Purchasing Parameters — MOST LIKELY

In your SPRO config table EHSWAC_ENTAMBW (configured via Specify Parameters for Purchasing / WACM30), the field KNTTP (Account Assignment Category) is either:

  • Blank — not filled at all
  • Set to a value that conflicts with your cost centre setup

When KNTTP is blank, the FM EHSWA_112_DATA_FOR_PO still tries to build a POACCOUNT row but cannot assign a proper item number to it — resulting in item number 00000 being passed to BAPI.

 

Table check in NES : While executing the transaction WACM30 or in SM30 – table :

 

 

 

Please do needful to create PO from WAE02 transaction with out any errors.

entry.title: Document Generation – Temporary vs. Home Address Logic
entry.content:
Hi all,

I would kindly ask for your advice regarding Document Generation in SuccessFactors.

The requirement from the client is that in the contract the temporary address should be printed if it exists; otherwise, the home address should be used.

I am using Document Generation with a mapping rule, and I tried to build a business rule to return only one address to the template. However, I am facing issues due to the fact that Address Information is a multi-record object, and the mapping rule:

  • processes each address record separately,
  • does not support proper filtering (for example, “get first record where address type = temporary”),
  • and as a result I either:
    • get multiple values error, or
    • always get the home address instead of temporary, depending on how the rule is structured.

Due to project constraints, I cannot use:

  • Employee Central Business Rules for pre-calculating the address, or
  • advanced template logic (loops/conditions) in the document template.

Has anyone faced a similar limitation and found a reliable workaround within Document Generation Mapping Rules only, that ensures:

  • only one address is returned, and
  • preference is given to the temporary address if it exists?

Any advice or best practice would be highly appreciated.

Thank you in advance, Urška

SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central - Employee Profile

entry.title: 🇸🇬 Build AI services using SAP CAP and Generative AI Hub (Singapore)
entry.content:
This SAP CodeJam event is on the topic of Build AI services using the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model, SAP AI Core and Generative AI Hub. It is beneficial if you have some basic knowledge about JavaScript as we are using the SAP Cloud SDK for AI with the JavaScript variant.

Please note that this is an advanced SAP CAP CodeJam and it requires participants to have basic understanding of the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model. 

This CodeJam introduces attendees to aspects of exposing generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) capabilities to execute tasks using a given business context.

During this event, you will become acquainted with the theory behind GenAI, SAP AI Core, SAP AI Launchpad, and learn how to use the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model to expose AI capabilities through your own OData service.

While working through the exercises, you will become comfortable creating CDS-based service definitions and integrating them into the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) and SAP AI Core Foundation Models.

Prerequisites

  • Bring your own laptop with power adapter and have a supported Web browser such as Google Chrome or Firefox installed on your laptop.
  • It can be helpful to bring a tablet or second laptop, if you have.
  • We will be using the primary environment as described in the prerequisites here https://github.com/SAP-samples/codejam-cap-llm/blob/main/prerequisites.md#prerequisites

Location and Date

This is a free in-person event and is planned for Wednesday, August 26, in Singapore from 10:00 AM to 4:00 PM local time. The event's language and content will be in English. 

Event Address: SAP Asia Pte Ltd.30 Pasir Panjang Rd, Singapore 117440, Located in: Mapletree Business City.

Google Maps: https://maps.app.goo.gl/6ZEMBPXsthyshYH2A

  • A huge thank you to Mark9 Solutions for hosting this event.
  • The instructors are SAP Developer Advocates @ajay_soreng  and @shrinivasan_neelamegam 

Please note that spaces are limited! We can only accommodate 35 attendees. If you want to attend, register by clicking the Yes button in the Will you be attending? section above to the right. Approximately 2 weeks prior to the event, you will receive an email to confirm your attendance, along with further instructions and details.

For more information on SAP CodeJam events, head over to the SAP CodeJam group.

entry.title: Re: SAP Business One AddOnIdentifier property distribution
entry.content:
Hi,
Yes, that's correct. Once SAP issues your BASIS license, you'll get the Solution license key enter that in the Add-On Identifier Generator with type set to "Solution," and it generates your Solution Add-On Identifier. Embed that in the AddonIdentifier property in your code, compile once, and that same build ships to all your clients without any further recompiling.
Just double-check with SAP the exact field name/format they expect at that point, since it can vary slightly by SDK version but conceptually, that's the complete flow.

entry.title: Re: SAP COMMERCE - How is the built-in anonymous user pas...
entry.content:
Dear @stefaniaZ ,

SAP Commerce does not handle the anonymous user's password rehashing automatically through any internal background process. The password.encoding.auto.update.enabled mechanism is login-triggered and therefore does not apply to the anonymous user.

When password.encoding.auto.update.enabled is set to true, user passwords are automatically rehashed to the current default algorithm (Argon2) upon a successful interactive login.
The anonymous user is explicitly excluded from this flow because it does not perform an interactive login — no plaintext password is ever provided during its authentication. Since rehashing requires the platform to validate the plaintext password at login time, the anonymous user's stored hash cannot be upgraded through this mechanism.

Could you refer the below kba and follow the steps as specified.

https://me.sap.com/notes/3701165 : Error "Failed to initialize the 'anonymous' user" during deployment with migrate data while upgrading to 2211.47

Cause
In the 2211.47 commerce version, the plaintext password encoder was removed. And the "encrypted" value stored in the encodedPassword field of anonymous users is actually misformatted pseudo-encrypted data. When the TAE layer attempts to decrypt this misformatted encrypted value, it explicitly throws an error.

Resolution
To resolve this issue, please follow the steps below:

Deploy the previous Commerce version in data migration mode.
After deployment is complete, update the Password Encoding:
Go to Backoffice >> User >> Customers
Search "Anonymous"
Set argon2 as the password encoder and set a new password for the Anonymous user.
Save

 

Regards,

Prasanth

SAP Support

entry.title: Blog#6: How to add additional test acceptances in the Feature process in SAP Cloud ALM
entry.content:
In my last post, I shared how the SAP Cloud ALM UI can be tailored to what your team needs to see. This time it is about another part of customization: how to bring your own approval steps into the standard SAP Cloud ALM processes.

Every team has its own way of approving work. Some need a UAT sign-off before testing can be confirmed, others need a compliance check before a Feature moves forward. With pony, additional approval steps can be added directly to the standard SAP Cloud ALM artifacts, similar to the assignment blocks you may know from SAP Solution Manager.



Let's be specific: A "Test Acceptance" block with two approval steps for the Feature process

Here is a concrete example from one of my projects. On the Feature, a new "Test Acceptance" section holds two approval steps that become active when the Feature reaches the status "In Testing". The first step is assigned to a specific user, the second to a group called "Test Coordinators". The two steps run sequentially, so the second one only opens after the first is approved.

Test Acceptance


As soon as the Feature moves into the testing phase, the first approval step opens automatically. The Feature status cannot move forward to "Tested" until both sign-offs are in place. If someone tries to skip ahead, a clear error message reminds them that the UAT approval is still pending. The check is preventive, not reactive.

Preventive check


When the first approver gives their sign-off, the procedure moves on to the second step. Group approvals work the same way as single-user approvals: any member of the "Test Coordinators" group can grant the sign-off. Once both approvals are done, the test can be confirmed and the Feature can move forward.

Approve Steps

 

Approvals that match the way your team works

The example above uses two sequential steps with one single user and one group. The approval procedure can be shaped to fit very different scenarios:

  • Single user or group: A step can be assigned to a specific person or to an approval group (e.g., "Test Coordinators")
  • Sequential or parallel: Steps can run one after the other, or all at the same time
  • Triggered by status: Each procedure becomes active at a specific status value of the artifact
  • Configurable rejection handling: On rejection, the procedure can go back one step, jump back to the first step, stay on the current step for another decision, or be cancelled entirely
  • Email notifications: Approvers can be notified by email as soon as it is their turn


How it is configured, without code

The "Approval Procedures" are configured with a graphical editor. No developer required.

In the pony administration, we find two new tiles: "Approval Groups" and "Approval Procedures".

Two new tiles in the pony administration

 

The "Approval Groups" tile holds the groups of users that can be used as approvers. In our example, the group "Test Coordinators" has been created. New users can be added to this group at any time, so that multiple people are allowed to approve.

In the "Approval Procedures" tile, we see all configured procedures, in our example the "UAT Approval" on the Feature. The editor of each procedure follows three logical steps:

  • Step 1 - What to approve: choose the artifact (e.g., Feature), give the procedure a name and a label, and add a short description
  • Step 2 - Who approves and how: pick a single user or an approval group, set the mode (sequential or parallel), and define at which status value of the artifact the procedure becomes active
  • Step 3 - What happens after: decide what happens on a rejection (go back one step, jump to the first step, stay on the step, or cancel the entire procedure), and whether approvers are notified by email when it is their turn

Details of an approval procedure


Once a procedure is activated, the configured approval steps appear in the 'Test Acceptance' section of every matching artifact.

In summary: approval processes can be tailored to the way your team really works. Each artifact gets exactly the approval steps it needs, with the right people, in the right order, and with the right consequences on rejection. The Feature continues to use the standard SAP Cloud ALM process, complemented by the additional sign-offs your governance requires.

In my next post, I will share how Template Protection can be set up in SAP Cloud ALM with pony, so your standard templates stay consistent and protected from unintended changes.

entry.title: Re: Updating Sales Order Header and Sales Order Item using Business Object Framework(Cleancore Appro

entry.title: How does IBT1 field Direction work?
entry.content:
Please just point me to the legally required comprehensive documentation for this product (lol).

Table IBT1 has a field "Direction". According to some documentation I found extern (as usual), that field can be 0 for In, 1 for Out and 2 for Allocated. But how exactly does this work? What exactly is "allocated" vs. out and what BaseTypes of documents yield what direction? Is it safe to assume that allocated means 'still in house' while out means 'in delivery'? Is it safe to assume that a BaseType of 15 always corresponds to a Direction of 1? How is it handled if someone gets charged for delivering something?

entry.title: SAP Utilities Customer Engagement: How to assign existing TCODE Action Profile to a custom button?
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

I am working in SAP Utilities Customer Engagement / CRM WebClient UI and I need advice on the correct way to reuse an existing transaction/action profile on another view.

Current situation

In Customer Engagement, there is an existing Transaction dropdown/menu which contains actions such 

 
Abschlagsplan anlegen > Create Budget Billing Plan
Abschlagsplan anzeigen > Display Budget Billing Plan
Abschlagsplan ändern > Edit Budget Billing Plan
Zahlungsplan anzeigen > Display  Payment plan
Zahlungsplan ändern > Edit Payment plan
Zahlungsschema anzeigen > Display Payment Scheme
Zahlungsschema ändern > Edit Payment Scheme
 

 

These entries are maintained in an action profile:

 
Action Profile: BBP_TX
Action Type: TCODE Transaction-Launcher

The actions include entries such as:

 
BBP_TX_DISPLAY
BBP_TX_CHANGE
Z_BBP_TX_CREATE
BBP_PP_TX_DISPLAY
BBP_PP_TX_CHANGE
BBP_PS_TX_DISPLAY
BBP_PS_TX_CHANGE
 

I confirmed that the actions are configured as TCODE Transaction-Launcher actions.

 

 

Custom WebUI enhancement

I enhanced component/view:

 
Component: IUICBBP
Enhancement: Z_UI_.....
View/Window: IUICBBP/Change
Implementation class: ZL_IUICBBP_CHANGE_IMPL
 

I added a new toolbar button in:

 
ABAP
 
IF_BSP_WD_TOOLBAR_CALLBACK~GET_BUTTONS
 

Example:

ABAP
 
METHOD if_bsp_wd_toolbar_callback~get_buttons.
 
rt_buttons = super->if_bsp_wd_toolbar_callback~get_buttons( ).
 
APPEND VALUE #(
id = 'Z_ABSCH'
text = 'Abschlagsplan anlegen'
enabled = abap_true
type = 'MENUITEM'
on_select = 'CLICK_ABSCH'
on_click = 'CLICK_ABSCH'
) TO rt_buttons.
 
ENDMETHOD.
 

 

The button can be added on another view in Customer Engagement , but I do not know how to connect it with the existing action profile / transaction launcher actions. For now the button is "empty" - no action happen when clicking on it/no drop down menu with the desired action profile/transactions.

 

My question

What is the correct approach to reuse the existing transaction/action profile on this new view/button?

  1. Is there a standard CE/WebUI method/API to render or execute an action profile such as BBP_TX from IF_BSP_WD_TOOLBAR_CALLBACK~GET_BUTTONS?

What is the correct way to proceed?

Additional details

I could not find the existing "Transaktionen" button via F2 technical information in Customer Engagement. It seems to be generated by the Customer Engagement form/action profile framework rather than directly by BSP view code.

I also checked the normal save button path, and it only calls the BOL transaction save logic, for example:

 
ABAP
 
EH_ONSAVE
-> CL_ISUBBP_CUCOBBP_IMPL->SAVE_BPLAN
-> lr_trans->save( )
...

So this seems unrelated to the transaction launcher menu.

What I need

I would like to know the recommended SAP standard approach:

  • Can an action profile be attached to a custom WebUI toolbar button?

Any guidance, standard class/method names, or customizing path would be appreciated.

Thank you.

entry.title: Re: Reference notification and PO are not linked to the o...
entry.content:
Good day @Teja36 

Thank you for your question.

In Public Cloud, the custom follow up actions Z0000001 and Z0000002 can create the purchase order and the new quality notification, but this does not automatically create a relationship in the original notification’s standard document flow.

The BAdI FOA_EXECUTION_FOR_TASK is provided to execute the logic of the follow up action. Note that the action is executed when the quality notification is saved and that its execution is documented by a notification task.

Here are references for more guidance

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/d1e58be39d884a0dbf75a7526a9acbf4/45a31543e01f401e954e05bea103864f.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/d1e58be39d884a0dbf75a7526a9acbf4/fc8dfd714048409fb544921b94278a55.html?locale=en-US

So, in simple terms:

  • The configuration of Z0000001 and Z0000002 controls which custom logic is executed.
  • The BAdI creates or updates the required business object according to your implementation.
  • The notification task records that the follow up action was executed.

Please verify the current implementation as follows:

  • In Custom Logic app, open the implementation for Follow Up Action Execution for Quality Task.                    
  • Confirm that the implementation is active and that the filter value matches Z0000001 or Z0000002.
  • Execute the action from the source quality notification and save the notification.
  • Check the task created by the action box.

If your requirement is specifically to display the created PO or notification as a node in the standard document flow, you will have to raise an SAP Support and provide necessary details.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.content:
Dear Juegen,

Thank you for your prompt reply. This also means it also depends on customer as well right? If during the conversion from S/4HANA on-premise to SAP Cloud ERP Private, they decide to implement changes of LE-WM to either Stock Room Mgt., or Basic/ Advance EWM depending on their current functions. Since the conversion timeline is kind of mini-project that process changed, testing, training activities can be done in that timeline. 

But if they are relying on current functions on LE-WM, they have sometimes to plan for a replacement, perhaps with the next S/4 versions on 2027 or 2029 release.

Best regards,

Prinya

entry.title: Beyond Single-Company: Configuring Multi-Company PM Order Replication to SAP FSM
entry.content:
Contents

  1. Introduction & Business Context
  2. Why Multi-Company?
  3. How It Works — The Big Picture
  4. Step-by-Step Configuration Guide
  5. Controlling the FSM Planning Board
  6. Extensibility
  7. Monitoring & Error Recovery
  8. Summary

  

1.   Introduction & Business Context

SAP S/4HANA Plant Maintenance (PM) and SAP Field Service Management (FSM) are designed to work together as one end-to-end maintenance execution platform. When a planner raises a PM order in S/4HANA — for a breakdown repair, a preventive inspection, or a scheduled overhaul — that order is automatically pushed to FSM, where a dispatcher assigns it to the right field technician. The technician receives the work on their mobile device, executes it on-site, and the completion confirmation flows back to S/4HANA —

closing the loop without manual re-entry.

A companion blog post covers this integration for organisations with a single FSM company. This blog addresses a more common enterprise scenario: a single SAP system serving multiple business units, regions, or subsidiaries, each managed by a separate FSM company.

 

  

2. Why Multi-Company?


    SAP FSM supports a single-company model where all field service data lives in one place.
    While authorizations can be used to restrict what each country or business unit user sees,
    this still means all their data — customers, orders, technicians, activities — is loaded
    into and managed within that single company. For many enterprises this is not desirable:
    different subsidiaries may have different contracts, different service processes, different
    FSM configurations, or strict data segregation requirements. Multi-company support
    allows organisations to segregate their business and data into separate FSM companies,
    each operating independently with its own configuration, workforce, and dispatching logic —
    while still being driven from a single S/4HANA system.
 


    Beyond data segregation, multi-company support also enables:
 

Capability

What it means in practice

Automatic routing

PM orders are routed to the correct FSM company based on the planning plant and
            order type — no manual steps, no middleware transformation needed.

Independent filter rules

One subsidiary may push orders to FSM as soon as they are Released; another may
            prefer to wait until Ready for Scheduling. Each company configures this independently.

Independent code-list mappings

Status codes, service call types, problem types, priorities, and regions can all
            differ between FSM companies to match local FSM configurations.

Custom routing for complex scenarios

For organisations where plant and order type alone are not enough to determine the
            target FSM company, a custom BAdI provides full programmatic control.

 

 

3. How It Works — The Big Picture


    Every time a PM order is released and saved in S/4HANA — on creation or any subsequent
    update — the system evaluates whether and where to replicate it to FSM. The steps are:
 

Step

What happens

1   Trigger

PM order save detected. Replication check begins in the background.

2   Company routing

The order's planning plant and order type are matched against configuration to
            identify the responsible FSM company.

3   Relevance check

The order's current system status is compared against the configured filter rules.
            Only orders in a qualifying status proceed.

4   Field mapping

PM order data is translated into an FSM Service Call payload — subject, status,
            type, priority, region, activities, components, partners, and more.

5   Replication

The payload is sent asynchronously to FSM. FSM creates or updates the Service
            Call and its Activities accordingly.

6   Response & logging

The FSM response is evaluated and logged in SAP's Application Interface
            Framework (AIF), giving operations teams full visibility and restart capability.

 

Note:  Why asynchronous? Replication happens in the background via a queue —
    it does not hold up the PM order save transaction. Planners experience no delay;
    the FSM API call is processed independently after the order is committed in S/4HANA.

 

4. Step-by-Step Configuration Guide


    Configuration lives in two places in SPRO (transaction SPRO😞
 

  • Multi-company routing (Steps 1–5) is maintained via a single SPRO node that
          opens a view cluster. All sub-views are navigated through the
          Dialog Structure panel on the left-hand side of the screen.
         
    SPRO Node: Service → Integration with Other SAP Solutions → SAP Field Service Management →
    Configure Multiple Company Replication
            

  • Code-list mappings & region (Steps 6–7) are direct child nodes under
          a separate path with no additional grouping:
         
           SPRO Node: Plant Maintenance and Customer Service → Integration with SAP Field Service Management 

 

4.1. Define S/4 FSM Companies


      Create a logical identifier for each FSM company you want to replicate to. This short key
      — for example COMP_NORTH or COMP_SOUTH — becomes the
      master reference that all subsequent configuration links back to:
      FSM account details, plant assignments, filter rules, and code-list mappings all
      reference this identifier.
   

SPRO:  Configure Multiple Company Replication → Dialog Structure:Define S/4HANA FSM Company

 

 

4.2. Assign FSM Account & Company Details


      For each S/4 FSM Company ID, enter the technical identifiers from your FSM tenant.
      These are found in the FSM Admin portal under
      Account → Account Information and Account → Companies.
   

Field

Description

Where to find in FSM Admin

FSM Account ID

Numeric identifier for the FSM tenant account

FSM Admin → Account Information

FSM Company ID

Numeric identifier for the specific FSM company

FSM Admin → Companies → select company → ID field

 

SPRO:  Configure Multiple Company Replication → Dialog Structure: Define S/4HANA FSM Company →Assign FSM Company Details

Note:  RFC destination is shared across all companies: The FSM Account ID
      and Company ID configured here are sent as HTTP request headers and can differ per S/4 FSM
      Company. However, the underlying RFC destination — which defines the FSM
      endpoint URL and OAuth profile — is always read from the global configuration
      ( CRMC_FSM_CONFIG ) and cannot be varied per company, either through the
      multi-company table or through the BAdI. This means all S/4 FSM companies must
      reside within the same FSM tenant — they are separate companies within one
      account, not companies on different FSM endpoints.

 

4.3. Assign Rule Type to the PM Replication Object


      Specify how the system determines which FSM company a PM order is routed to.
      The rule type is a configuration value that tells the system which routing strategy to
      apply. Two rule types are supported for PM orders:
   

Code

Name

When to choose it

01

Plant


            The target FSM company is determined by the order's planning plant
            and order type. This is the standard approach and covers
            the vast majority of customer scenarios.
         

07

Custom Logic


            A custom BAdI implementation determines the FSM company programmatically.
            Use this only when plant and order type alone are not sufficient — for example,
            when routing depends on a functional location attribute or a custom partner function.
            See Section 6 for details.
         

 

Set the Replication Object = PM and select the appropriate rule type.

SPRO:  Configure Multiple Company Replication → Dialog Structure:Assign Rule Type to Replication Object

 

4.4. Enable PM Replication per Company


      For each S/4 FSM Company ID, activate PM order replication by adding the
      replication object PM. This acts as a
      per-company master switch — if a company does not have PM enabled here,
      no PM orders will ever be routed to it, regardless of the plant configuration in Step 4.
   

SPRO:  Configure Multiple Company Replication → Dialog Structure:Assign Replication Object Details

 

 

  

4.5. Assign Relevant Plants — Rule Type 01 Only


      For plant-based routing, define which combination of S/4 FSM Company +
      Planning Plant + Order Type should route PM orders to which FSM company.
      This is the core routing table.
   

Important:  This routing configuration is maintained here under the multi-company replication IMG node. It is a separate entry point from the   single-company plant filter and must not be confused with it.

S/4 FSM Company

Planning Plant

Order Type

Routes to

COMP_NORTH

1000

PM01

Northern Operations FSM

COMP_SOUTH

2000

PM01

Southern Operations FSM

COMP_SOUTH

3000

PM02

Southern Operations FSM

 

SPRO:  Configure Multiple Company Replication → Dialog Structure: Assign Replication Object Details →Assign Relevant Plants

 

 

 

4.6. Configure Code-List Mappings & Filters


      SAP PM and FSM use different codes for the same business concepts. All configuration
      in this step uses SPRO node:
   

SPRO:  Plant Maintenance and Customer Service →Integration with SAP Field Service Management

Each item below corresponds to a separate child node in this path.

4.6.1. Define System Status to filter Maintenance Order replication


      Configure which S/4 system statuses should trigger or
      block replication for each company and order type. Each subsidiary
      independently decides at which point in the PM lifecycle orders flow to FSM.
   

Setting

Meaning

Order Status

The S/4 system status that activates this rule — e.g.
              Released, Ready for Scheduling,
              Technically Complete

Replication Type 1

Replicate when this status is active → order lands on the
              Dispatching Board in FSM

Replication Type 2

Block replication when this status is active

Replication Type 3

Replicate when this status is active → order lands on the
              Capacity Planning Board in FSM

 

SPRO:  Integration with SAP Field Service Management →Define System Status to filter Maintenance Order replication

 

 

4.6.2 Configure Mapping for Status Codelist


      Translates S/4 system statuses to FSM Service Call status codes. When multiple
      active statuses match simultaneously, the entry with the
      lowest configured priority number is applied.
      Example: S/4 Released → FSM status –2 (Ready to Plan).
   

SPRO:  Integration with SAP Field Service Management →Configure Mapping for Status Codelist

 

4.6.3. Configure Mapping for Service Call Type Codelist


      Maps the S/4 order type (e.g. PM01, PM02) to the FSM
      Service Call Type (e.g. Maintenance, Repair, Inspection).
      This determines how work is categorised in FSM and affects which service call
      templates and follow-up workflows are triggered.
   

SPRO:  Integration with SAP Field Service Management →Configure Mapping for Service Call Type Codelist

 

 

4.6.4. Configure Mapping for Problem Type Codelist


      Maps the S/4 maintenance activity type (e.g. Electrical, Mechanical,
      Preventive) to the FSM Problem Type. This helps dispatchers instantly
      understand the nature of the job and assign a technician with the right skill set.
   

SPRO:  Integration with SAP Field Service Management →Configure Mapping for Problem Type Codelist

 

 

4.6.5. Configure Mapping for Priority Codelist


      Maps S/4 PM priority codes to FSM priority values (HIGH, MEDIUM, LOW). A separate
      Emergency Priority field is also available — when an emergency order
      is processed (identified by its processing context), this value overrides the standard
      priority mapping, ensuring urgent work is immediately visible to the FSM dispatcher.
   

SPRO:  Integration with SAP Field Service Management →Configure Mapping for Priority Codelist

 

4.6.6. Set Default Business Partner (Customer)


      Defines a fallback customer (Business Partner) per FSM company, used when the PM order
      does not carry a Ship-To party. FSM requires a business partner on every Service Call,
      so this default prevents replication failures for orders that have no customer reference.
   

SPRO:  Integration with SAP Field Service Management →Set Default Business Partner (Customer)

 

4.6.7. Configure Region


      In FSM, a Region links each Activity to a geographical or organisational
      area, which drives scheduling availability, resource pooling, and address resolution.
      For PM integration, the region code is derived from S/4HANA data and included in
      every Activity sent to FSM. The source of that region code is configurable
      per FSM company, giving each subsidiary the flexibility to align
      the region model to how they have structured FSM.
   

SPRO:  Integration with SAP Field Service Management →Configure Region

 

 

 

5. Controlling the FSM Planning Board


    When a PM order arrives in FSM it lands on one of two planning boards, depending on how
    the filter rule was configured in Step 6. This determines how the dispatcher interacts
    with the work before a technician is assigned.
 

FSM Board

When it is used

What the dispatcher sees

Dispatching Board

Replication triggered from Released status (Replication Type 1).
            The order is ready for immediate technician assignment.

Service Call appears directly on the Dispatching Board — the dispatcher can
            drag and drop it onto a technician's schedule straight away.

Capacity Planning Board

Replication triggered from Ready for Scheduling status
            (Replication Type 3). The order needs resource-level planning first.

Service Call appears on the Capacity Planning Board for workload balancing
            and resource allocation before moving to Dispatching.

 

 

6. Extensibility


    When rule type 07 (Custom Logic) is configured for the PM replication
    object, the system invokes BAdI CRMS4_FSM_RELV_COMP_DETERM to determine
    which FSM company and account a PM order should be routed to. This gives full programmatic
    control over company routing for scenarios that cannot be handled by plant and order type
    alone.
 

BAdI: CRMS4_FSM_RELV_COMP_DETERM

Method: DETERMINE_RELVNT_COMP

When to implement


    Use this BAdI when the target FSM company cannot be resolved from planning plant and order
    type alone. Common examples:
 

  • Routing based on a functional location hierarchy — e.g. all orders
            under a specific FL node go to a specific FSM company regardless of plant
  • Routing based on a custom partner function or responsible person on
            the PM order
  • Routing determined by an external system lookup or custom Z-field
  • Complex multi-level routing logic that changes dynamically at runtime

How it works


    The BAdI is called inside the replication class.
    Before the BAdI is called, any company and account values already resolved by the standard
    plant-based lookup are cleared. This means the BAdI has full responsibility
    for the result — it completely overrides the standard determination.
 


    The BAdI is retrieved with a filter on replication object PM,
    so a single enhancement spot can hold separate implementations for PM orders, Service Orders,
    Equipment, and other replication objects — each triggered independently without interference.
 

Interface

Parameter

Direction

Description

IT_RELEVANT_OBJ_ID

Importing (table)


          One row per order:
          • SRVCMGMTFSMREPLICATIONOBJECT = PM
          • SRVCMGMTFSMRPLCTNOBJID = maintenance order number
       

CT_RELEVANT_COMP

Changing (table)


          Your implementation must populate at minimum:
          • FSMCOMPANY — target FSM Company ID
          • FSMACCOUNT — target FSM Account ID
       

 

Important:  Important: If CT_RELEVANT_COMP is left empty or an exception
    is raised, replication is silently skipped for that order — no error is
    written to AIF. Always ensure your implementation populates at least one row with a valid
    FSM Company ID and Account ID.

 

7. Monitoring & Error Recovery


    Every replication attempt — successful or failed — is recorded in SAP's
    Application Interface Framework (AIF). This gives operations
    teams complete visibility into what was sent to FSM, what FSM responded,
    and the ability to restart any failed message after the root cause is resolved —
    all without developer involvement.
 

From the AIF monitor, teams can:

  • See exactly which PM orders were replicated and when
  • Inspect the full message payload sent to FSM and the FSM response received
  • Restart failed messages with a single action — for example after a
            missing region in FSM has been created, or a connectivity issue has been resolved
  • Receive automatic alerts when errors occur, via configurable
            recipient groups — ensuring nothing fails silently

AIF Key

Value

Namespace

/FSMPM

ORDER_OUT

bgRFC Interface for PM Order replication to FSM

ORD_CRT_IN

Maintenance Order Inbound on create

ORDER_IN

Maintenance Order Inbound

ORDEROP_IN

Maintenance Order Operation Inbound

CONFIRM_IN

Maintenance Order Confirmation Inbound

Transaction

/AIF/ERR

 

 

8. Summary


    Multi-company PM FSM integration enables enterprises to run a unified SAP system while
    keeping each business unit's field service operations fully independent. Here is
    what it delivers at a glance:
 

Capability

Benefit

Automatic plant-based routing

Each plant's PM orders flow to the correct FSM company without manual steps
            or middleware logic.

Per-company status filters

Each subsidiary independently decides at which PM lifecycle stage orders
            are pushed to FSM.

Per-company code-list mappings

Status, priority, service call type, and problem type mappings are fully
            independent per FSM company.

Flexible region derivation

Region codes can be sourced from the order work center, operation work center,
            or planner group — independently per FSM company.

FSM planning board control

Orders land on the Dispatching Board or Capacity Planning Board based on
            the PM process used, with full Phase Model and emergency order support.

Custom company routing (Rule Type 07)

A BAdI provides full programmatic control for complex routing scenarios
            that go beyond plant and order type.

Full backward compatibility

If no multi-company configuration is present, the system falls back
            automatically to single-company mode — no migration required.

 

 

References

  • Integrating SAP S/4HANA Plant Maintenance Orders with SAP FSM

entry.title: Re: Custom Field and Logic from Scratch
entry.content:
Hi Prabhat @PM_iiits , it's totally okay to contribute to the community. We cannot search on LinkedIn for specific content like we can do here. That makes the community more useful to find structured content. I have two suggestions -

1. Please post only the content which you find unique (not shared by anyone else) in the SAP community.
2. Keep it structured. Say you prepared a PPT which you shared on LinkedIn, you can copy the content and structure it to make it a readable blog. Having  only images as a blog is not a good structured blog.

Thank you for your contributions.

Sachin

entry.title: Re: Production Order Status Not Updating to DLV
entry.content:
Why DLV is not triggered is because, DLV (Delivered) status is set only when a Goods Receipt (GR) is posted against the production order. Confirmation in CO11N (yield + scrap) updates the order progress, but it does not update the delivery status. The system still expects a GR posting (movement type 101 or similar) to bring the order to DLV. Scrap booking (movement type 531 or 551, depending on setup) does not count as a delivery. Scrap is treated separately and does not trigger DLV.
To meet your requirement, enable automatic GR at confirmation or ensure users post GR manually.
